name: Confirmation bias
summary: The tendency to notice, seek, and remember evidence that supports the story you already prefer more readily than evidence that threatens it.
mechanism: Once a hypothesis feels plausible, attention and memory become selective. Friendly evidence feels weighty while counterevidence feels weak, atypical, or unimportant.
distortion: It makes the evidence stream look more one-sided than it really is.
defaultMove: Protect the current belief by sampling supportive cases first.
commonTrigger: Identity-charged topics and early hunches formed under uncertainty.
firstCountermove: Write down what evidence would seriously lower confidence before searching for more support.
signals: You can recall supporting examples much faster than disconfirming ones.
Counterexamples are dismissed before they are investigated.
Searching stops as soon as the preferred story fits.
spotIt: Ask which live alternative explanations were actively tested rather than merely mentioned.
Check whether the evidence list is mostly made of confirming cases.
Notice whether disconfirming data is being screened out on quality grounds only after it points the wrong way.
slowIt: Generate two rival hypotheses before collecting the next round of evidence.
Assign one person to build the strongest countercase.
Use the same evidence standards for friendly and unfriendly cases.
reframeIt: Treat belief revision as progress rather than loss.
Ask which result would most surprise you and go look there first.
Reword the question from 'How do I defend this?' to 'What best explains the full pattern?'

name: Motivated reasoning
summary: The tendency to use reasoning as a defense lawyer for desired conclusions rather than as an impartial search for what is most likely true.
mechanism: Goals such as belonging, status, self-respect, or ideological loyalty steer which standards feel fair, which doubts feel urgent, and which explanations feel satisfying.
distortion: It quietly changes the burden of proof depending on which conclusion you want.
defaultMove: Scrutinize hostile claims hard while granting friendly claims easier passage.
commonTrigger: Topics tied to tribe, ego, moral identity, or prior public commitment.
firstCountermove: State the conclusion you do not want to be true and then spell out what evidence would nevertheless make it likeliest.
signals: Standards tighten only for one side of the question.
The emotional tone changes when one conclusion becomes personally costly.
The strongest opposing evidence is described vaguely while friendly evidence is described concretely.
spotIt: Look for asymmetry in skepticism: who gets grilled and who gets trusted quickly?
Check whether the evidence standard moves with the favored answer.
Notice whether the person's conclusion was psychologically attractive before the reasoning began.
slowIt: Argue the opposite side in writing until it sounds intellectually serious.
Use a precommitted checklist for what counts as adequate evidence.
Invite a critic to inspect the standards, not just the conclusion.
reframeIt: Treat revising a view as a gain in calibration, not a betrayal.
Ask what you would think if your out-group had made the same argument.
Separate personal identity from the fate of the proposition.

name: Availability heuristic
summary: The tendency to judge frequency, risk, or importance by how easily examples come to mind.
mechanism: Vivid, recent, repeated, or emotionally loaded cases are easier to retrieve, so they feel more common and more representative than they are.
distortion: It turns memory accessibility into a mistaken proxy for prevalence or probability.
defaultMove: Let the easiest recalled example stand in for the whole distribution.
commonTrigger: Recent news cycles, memorable anecdotes, dramatic failures, and repetition.
firstCountermove: Ask for a base rate or outside-view frequency before trusting the vivid example.
signals: One memorable case is doing most of the persuasive work.
The perceived risk changes sharply after recent exposure.
Examples are plentiful but representative data is thin.
spotIt: Ask whether the current estimate is based on retrieval ease rather than a counted sample.
Check whether a recent dramatic event is distorting the sense of frequency.
Look for missing quiet cases that never became memorable.
slowIt: List the reference class before discussing the anecdote.
Search for denominator information, not just striking cases.
Use longer time windows than the last week or last headline.
reframeIt: Treat ease of recall as a clue about salience, not about rate.
Replace 'I can picture it' with 'How often does it actually happen?'
Ask what a boring spreadsheet would likely show.

name: Negativity bias
summary: The tendency to give bad news, threats, criticism, and losses more psychological weight than equally sized positives.
mechanism: Potential harms command attention quickly because organisms that miss threats pay steep costs. That makes negative information sticky, memorable, and behavior-shaping.
distortion: It can make the whole environment look worse, riskier, or more hostile than the total evidence supports.
defaultMove: Overweight what could go wrong and underweight quiet competence or improvement.
commonTrigger: Conflict, uncertainty, public criticism, and feedback environments that highlight errors.
firstCountermove: Force a count of what is going right before deciding how diagnostic the negative signal really is.
signals: One bad interaction outweighs many ordinary or good ones.
The discussion keeps circling around dangers without proportioning them.
Neutral events are encoded as mildly negative by default.
spotIt: Ask whether the current emotional weight matches the actual rate or magnitude of the problem.
Check whether positive base-line performance has disappeared from view.
Notice whether a criticism is being treated as more informative than equivalent praise.
slowIt: Count positive, neutral, and negative cases separately.
Review trend lines instead of the single worst episode.
Distinguish serious risks from ordinary friction.
reframeIt: Ask what a neutral observer would score here.
Treat vivid bad news as a signal to inspect, not an automatic verdict.
Make room for the possibility that the system is mixed rather than globally bad.

name: Anchoring effect
summary: The tendency for the first salient number, frame, or option to pull later estimates toward itself even when it is arbitrary or weakly relevant.
mechanism: Initial values set a starting point, and later adjustment is usually too small. The mind mistakes a convenient first reference for an informative one.
distortion: It biases negotiation, forecasting, pricing, and planning by shrinking the range of considered outcomes.
defaultMove: Start from the first offered value and adjust insufficiently away from it.
commonTrigger: Opening offers, first forecasts, suggested budgets, and round numbers.
firstCountermove: Record an independent estimate before exposing yourself to any outside number.
signals: Different opening numbers produce different 'reasonable' endpoints.
People defend estimates they generated only after seeing a suggested figure.
The discussion keeps orbiting a first number that no one has justified.
spotIt: Ask what the estimate would have been if a different opening number had appeared first.
Check whether the anchor is actually evidence or merely the first thing mentioned.
Look for narrow revisions that never escape the initial frame.
slowIt: Generate your own range before seeing the offered anchor.
Use outside-view distributions to set a fresh range.
Require a written reason for every major adjustment.
reframeIt: Treat the first number as noise unless it earns authority.
Ask which variables actually determine the answer.
Work from reference classes outward rather than from the anchor inward.

name: Overconfidence effect
summary: The tendency to be more certain about judgments, forecasts, or abilities than the evidence warrants.
mechanism: Coherent stories feel informative, familiarity feels like mastery, and feedback is often too sparse or noisy to recalibrate confidence reliably.
distortion: It produces narrow forecasts, premature closure, and risky commitments that look justified mainly because they feel fluent.
defaultMove: Confuse clarity of feeling with strength of evidence.
commonTrigger: Fast intuitive judgment, expertise in adjacent domains, and weak feedback loops.
firstCountermove: Translate confidence into a numeric range or probability and then compare it to real outcomes later.
signals: Forecast ranges are too tight for the actual uncertainty.
Questions are answered quickly with little discussion of downside cases.
Past misses are remembered as near misses rather than calibration failures.
spotIt: Ask how often this level of confidence has actually been right in comparable cases.
Check whether uncertainty has been compressed into a single-point forecast.
Notice whether disconfirming scenarios were seriously modeled.
slowIt: Use calibrated ranges instead of point estimates.
Run a premortem before major commitments.
Track forecast scores over time rather than trusting confidence impressions.
reframeIt: Treat uncertainty as a feature of the world, not a defect in your thinking.
Replace 'I know' with 'My current best range is...'
Ask what would have to be true for the confident view to fail.

name: Sunk cost effect
summary: The tendency to keep investing in a losing path because of what has already been spent, even when the forward-looking case has weakened.
mechanism: People hate waste, identity loss, and public admission of error. Past investment becomes psychologically fused with current justification.
distortion: It keeps projects, habits, and strategies alive for backward-looking reasons rather than forward-looking ones.
defaultMove: Treat past spending as a reason to continue rather than as an unrecoverable cost.
commonTrigger: Public commitments, long-running projects, and high personal ownership.
firstCountermove: Ask what you would do if the past investment had been made by someone else and could not be recovered.
signals: The defense of the project begins with how much has already been spent.
Exit feels like admitting humiliation rather than changing course.
Stopping rules were never defined before the investment started.
spotIt: Ask whether any forward-looking reason remains once prior costs are ignored.
Check whether continuation is being justified by pride, face, or sunk effort.
Look for the missing stop rule.
slowIt: Review options from today forward only.
Create explicit continuation criteria before the next round of investment.
Let a less invested reviewer make the retain-or-exit recommendation.
reframeIt: Treat exit as conservation of future resources, not disrespect for past effort.
Ask what course would maximize value from this point onward.
Normalize stopping as evidence of governance rather than weakness.

name: Status quo bias
summary: The tendency to prefer the current option, default, or inherited arrangement simply because it is the current option, default, or inherited arrangement.
mechanism: Change carries cognitive cost, anticipated regret, and responsibility. The existing state feels safer because its risks are already normalized.
distortion: It disguises omission as neutrality and can make inferior defaults look prudent.
defaultMove: Keep the current arrangement unless the alternative looks overwhelmingly better.
commonTrigger: Complex choices, uncertain outcomes, and systems with sticky defaults.
firstCountermove: Reframe the current default as just another option that needs to earn its place.
signals: The defense of the current setup is mostly that it is familiar.
Changing the default is treated as riskier than leaving it untouched without equal scrutiny.
Opportunity costs of inaction barely enter the conversation.
spotIt: Ask whether the current state is being evaluated or merely inherited.
Check whether inaction is being mistaken for a cost-free move.
Look for asymmetry in how regrets are imagined.
slowIt: Compare the default against a fresh outside option side by side.
Make the costs of staying visible, not just the costs of changing.
Rotate who is responsible for defending the default.
reframeIt: Treat defaults as hypotheses, not verdicts.
Ask what you would choose if no option were currently installed.
Remember that untouched systems also create winners, losers, and risks.

name: Fundamental attribution error
summary: The tendency to explain other people's behavior too quickly in terms of character while underweighting situational pressures and constraints.
mechanism: Behavior is vivid; context is often hidden. The mind reaches for trait stories because they feel coherent and socially portable.
distortion: It turns local behavior into overly global character judgments.
defaultMove: Read the act as a window into the person more than into the situation.
commonTrigger: Conflict, disappointment, public mistakes, and thin background knowledge.
firstCountermove: List three situational explanations before settling on a trait explanation.
signals: A single bad action quickly becomes a statement about what the person is like.
Context is treated as excuse-making only after the blame story is in place.
Your own behavior is explained situationally while others' behavior is explained dispositionally.
spotIt: Ask what constraints, incentives, or missing information the person may have been under.
Check whether the explanation would change if the actor were yourself or an ally.
Notice whether the leap from act to trait is faster than the evidence allows.
slowIt: Separate observed behavior, inferred motives, and character judgments into different columns.
Investigate situational pressures before discussing personality.
Look for repeated cross-situational patterns before making stable trait claims.
reframeIt: Replace 'That shows who they are' with 'That is one behavior under one set of conditions.'
Treat context as causal information, not as automatic exoneration.
Remember that opaque situations create harsh person-level stories.

name: Halo effect
summary: The tendency for one salient positive or negative impression to spill over into unrelated judgments about a person, product, or institution.
mechanism: The mind likes coherence. Once something feels admirable, attractive, competent, or impressive, neighboring traits are inferred too easily.
distortion: It makes broad evaluations look evidence-based when they are often driven by one glowing or tainted feature.
defaultMove: Project one strong impression across multiple unrelated dimensions.
commonTrigger: Charisma, prestige, beauty, eloquence, and one highly visible success or failure.
firstCountermove: Score the relevant traits separately before giving any overall rating.
signals: General praise or distrust arrives before trait-by-trait analysis.
One impressive quality is doing work in unrelated categories.
Weak evidence is accepted because the overall vibe feels strong.
spotIt: Ask which exact trait is supported and which extra traits are being smuggled in.
Check whether performance in one domain is being generalized too far.
Notice whether the judgment survives once labels and prestige cues are hidden.
slowIt: Separate criteria and rate them independently.
Use blind review when practical.
Force at least one disconfirming trait question before a global verdict.
reframeIt: Treat admiration as a starting prompt for inspection, not as a substitute for it.
Ask what evidence would be needed for each trait separately.
Remember that impressive people and objects are still mixtures.

name: Survivorship bias
summary: The tendency to learn from the visible winners while overlooking the invisible failures that dropped out of view.
mechanism: Success stories are easier to observe, celebrate, and circulate. Failed cases disappear, leaving the remaining sample badly distorted.
distortion: It makes risky paths look safer and strategies look stronger than they are.
defaultMove: Infer quality from the surviving sample without asking who is missing.
commonTrigger: Entrepreneurship stories, hiring narratives, investing, and institutional mythmaking.
firstCountermove: Ask what relevant failures are absent from the observed sample and why they are absent.
signals: The discussion focuses on people or projects that made it through the filter.
Failure data is unavailable, ignored, or treated as irrelevant.
Advice is drawn from admired outcomes without denominator information.
spotIt: Ask who did the same thing and did not survive to be counted.
Check whether the sample was filtered by success before the analysis began.
Look for missing attrition, dropout, or failure rates.
slowIt: Define the full reference population before analyzing the visible winners.
Search intentionally for failed comparators.
Report rates for entrants, not just for survivors.
reframeIt: Treat success stories as incomplete evidence, not as complete lessons.
Ask what the invisible cemetery of similar attempts would say.
Remember that visibility is often the consequence of success, not proof of replicable quality.

name: Base-rate neglect
summary: The tendency to underweight general prevalence information when vivid case-specific details are available.
mechanism: Concrete stories feel richer than abstract frequencies, so the mind overuses individuating detail and underuses prior probabilities.
distortion: It makes rare outcomes seem too plausible and ordinary outcomes seem too negligible.
defaultMove: Let the case description crowd out the prior odds.
commonTrigger: Diagnostic reasoning, legal reasoning, medical testing, and forecast updates with vivid narratives.
firstCountermove: Write down the outside-view base rate before discussing the specific case details.
signals: People talk richly about the case but cannot state the prior probability.
A vivid detail is treated as decisive without checking how common the outcome is overall.
Probability judgments drift far from the background rate for weak reasons.
spotIt: Ask what the base rate is for the outcome in the relevant reference class.
Check whether the case details are actually strong enough to swamp the prior.
Notice whether the narrative feels more informative than it really is.
slowIt: State the prior explicitly before updating.
Use simple likelihood-ratio language if full Bayes is too heavy.
Compare the vivid case against several equally vivid alternatives with different base rates.
reframeIt: Treat priors as part of the evidence, not as background wallpaper.
Ask how surprised you should be by this case if the base rate were true.
Remember that unusual stories happen inside ordinary distributions.

name: Hindsight bias
summary: The tendency, after an outcome is known, to see it as having been more obvious or predictable than it actually was beforehand.
mechanism: Once the ending is in place, memory edits the uncertainty out of the original situation. The path to the outcome becomes narratively smoother than it was in real time.
distortion: It destroys calibration and learning by making past uncertainty vanish.
defaultMove: Rewrite the old decision problem as if the ending had always been visible.
commonTrigger: Postmortems, political analysis, investing, sports talk, and any outcome with a strong public narrative.
firstCountermove: Use a decision journal or timestamped notes to recover what was actually known at the time.
signals: After the event, people claim the signs were obvious without having documented them earlier.
Original uncertainty is remembered as much narrower than it was.
Alternative outcomes stop feeling live once the actual one happens.
spotIt: Ask what specific forecast was recorded before the outcome.
Check whether the person can distinguish 'makes sense now' from 'was predictable then'.
Look for timeline compression and certainty inflation.
slowIt: Keep written forecasts and assumptions.
Review the information set as it existed then, not what is known now.
Include alternative outcomes that were genuinely plausible at the time.
reframeIt: Treat past uncertainty as real even when the ending now feels tidy.
Ask what would have happened to your narrative if the outcome had gone differently.
Use hindsight as a prompt for model revision, not for self-flattering inevitability stories.

name: Outcome bias
summary: The tendency to judge the quality of a decision mainly by how things turned out rather than by the quality of the reasoning under the uncertainty that existed at the time.
mechanism: Outcomes are concrete and emotionally loud, while process quality is abstract and harder to see. Success launders shaky reasoning; failure stains good reasoning.
distortion: It confuses luck with judgment and makes postmortems misleading.
defaultMove: Score the decision by the ending instead of by the information and reasoning available before the ending.
commonTrigger: Performance reviews, investing, leadership evaluation, and public retrospective blame.
firstCountermove: Separate process review from outcome review and ask whether the same decision would be endorsed if the result had flipped.
signals: Good outcomes excuse bad process or bad outcomes eclipse solid process.
The review begins with the ending before reconstructing the decision context.
Luck is talked about only when it benefits the side we dislike.
spotIt: Ask whether the same reasoning would be praised if the outcome were worse.
Check whether process quality has been independently inspected.
Look for decisions that seem wise only because the coin landed heads.
slowIt: Run separate process and outcome scorecards.
Use premortems and decision journals to document the original reasoning.
Compare this case to others with similar process but different luck.
reframeIt: Treat outcomes as noisy feedback, not as pure verdicts on judgment.
Ask whether the process was robust under the uncertainty that actually existed.
Remember that good bets can lose and bad bets can win.
